intro: The biggest real distinction between New Relic and Last9 is straightforward: New Relic carries moderate lock-in while Last9 has low lock-in. New Relic sits at hybrid pricing with $0.30/GB (Standard), $49/core user, a free tier, enterprise plan available and carries moderate lock-in, while Last9 sits at pricing from $1150, enterprise plan available and carries low lock-in. New Relic is a fit when you need microservices performance monitoring — typical scenarios include microservices performance monitoring. Last9 is aimed at microservices performance monitoring, with workloads like microservices performance monitoring as the common case. The honest trade-off: New Relic is explicitly not for simple, static projects; Last9 is not intended for simple, static projects. Lock-in is the factor most teams underestimate: New Relic and Last9 have different exit paths, and that shapes total cost of ownership over a two-to-three year horizon. Before committing, audit where each tool stores state, which APIs you depend on, and how painful a switch would be six months in.
quick_take: New Relic is for microservices performance monitoring; Last9 is for microservices performance monitoring; decide based on how much lock-in you can accept.
verdict_a: Choose New Relic if your primary workload is microservices performance monitoring and hybrid pricing ($0.30/GB (Standard), $49/core user) is acceptable.
verdict_b: Choose Last9 if your primary workload is microservices performance monitoring and low switching cost matters.

faq.q: Is New Relic cheaper than Last9?
faq.a: It depends on workload shape: New Relic lists $0.30/GB (Standard), $49/core user, while Last9 lists $1150. With different pricing models (hybrid vs subscription), the cheaper option flips based on seat count, usage volume, and whether you use the free tier. Do a per-workload estimate rather than comparing headline numbers.

faq.q: Can I migrate from New Relic to Last9?
faq.a: Migrating from New Relic to Last9 is doable but not automatic — New Relic carries moderate lock-in. Since both tools sit in the same category (Performance Monitoring / APM), the conceptual mapping is closer, but you still need to validate feature parity against your specific workload. Plan migration as a project, not a script: inventory integrations, test on non-production workloads, and keep New Relic running in parallel until Last9 is verified.

On our 0–5 developer experience scale, New Relic scores higher (5/5 vs 4/5 for Last9). The gap is one point — meaningful but not decisive, since DX is workflow-dependent. Still, score your own tasks: DX is subjective and tied to stack assumptions.

faq.q: Is Last9 a good alternative to New Relic?
faq.a: Last9 is in the same category as New Relic (Performance Monitoring / APM), so it's a reasonable alternative to evaluate. Both tools claim similar best-fit workloads on paper, so the real test is how each behaves under your specific constraints. Note that Last9 is explicitly not for simple, static projects — if that describes your workload, it's not a viable replacement.
